words.w: When Alaska burns, the smoke is the part that reaches everyone.
It sinks into the valleys, and for weeks, people breathe it.
So the state runs a model that forecasts the smoke two days out.
But that model has a habit. It reads the air too clean.
On bad days it undercounts the surface smoke by up to five times.
Here is why. A warm lid of air seals the valley.
It traps the smoke down low, where we breathe.
The model keeps drawing it thin and high.
So a team at the University of Alaska Fairbanks taught a second model to correct the first.
It learns the error and pulls the forecast down toward the air people breathe.
The gap shrinks. Five times too low becomes about two.
But this is a correction, not a cure. It sharpens the number.
And when a cloud hides a fire, the model can still miss it.
So the forecast lands closer, sooner.
And a person still decides when the air is safe to breathe.
